subject: freelancers
one_liner: A professional-grade receivables exchange where freelancers list, leverage, and liquidate their unpaid invoices.
brand_voice_notes: Institutional exchange gravitas applied to a person owed $850 for a logo. Prospectus language, fee schedules, risk disclosures that quietly make things worse. The product never acknowledges the joke; it files it.

hero.h1: Your invoices are already volatile. Trade them.
hero.sub: Float is a professional receivables exchange for the self-employed. List the invoice, watch the candles, and exit the position before the client exits the relationship.

features.title: The Receivables Order Book
features.blurb: List any Net-30 invoice on a live public order book and let the market price your client's intentions. Bids open at 61 cents on the dollar. That number is based on data.

features.title: 10x Hour Margin
features.blurb: Borrow against your own future billable hours at up to 10x leverage. Take a sick day and our risk engine liquidates your Thursday automatically. Discipline, outsourced.

features.title: Client Volatility Index
features.blurb: Every client gets a ticker and a candlestick chart built from their payment history. Set a stop-loss on the relationship. When it triggers, we send the goodbye email from a no-reply address.

pricing_tiers.name: Exposure
perks: One listed invoice at a time

pricing_tiers.name: Institutional
perks: A dedicated liquidation manager named Grant
API access to your own solvency
One in-person margin call per quarter

testimonials.quote: I shorted my own Q3 and it is the only position that has ever gone my way.
testimonials.name: Dana Okafor
testimonials.title: Freelance brand strategist, currently between rebrands

testimonials.quote: Float liquidated my biggest client before I got emotionally attached. I have not written a third follow-up email since March.
testimonials.name: Priya Raghunathan
testimonials.title: Independent UX researcher and part-time receivables day trader

testimonials.quote: My Henderson invoice hit an all-time high the same morning Henderson stopped opening my emails. I sold the top. I feel nothing.
testimonials.name: Marcus Bell
testimonials.title: Fractional CMO for four pre-revenue startups

images.rationale: Hero skipped: typographic bento prospectus carries the hero with a live market-snapshot tile, a photo would dilute the engraved-certificate read. Demo assets skipped: the terminal is charts, order books, and tape, all rendered UI, which the image guidelines ban. Full budget minus one went to three deadpan corporate headshots, one per named testimonial persona.

items.prompt: Deadpan corporate headshot, editorial photograph: a Black woman in her late thirties, a freelance brand strategist, wearing a deep evergreen #0E2A23 blazer, neutral unimpressed expression of quiet financial resolve, warm bone paper #F6F3EC studio backdrop, soft even annual-report lighting, engraved old-money prospectus mood, one focal subject, head and shoulders centered. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

Deadpan corporate headshot, editorial photograph: a South Asian woman in her early thirties, an independent UX researcher, wearing a cream blouse with a thin gold #C9971C pendant, calm clinical expression of someone who has automated her feelings, warm bone paper #F6F3EC studio backdrop, soft even annual-report lighting, engraved old-money prospectus mood, one focal subject, head and shoulders centered. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

Deadpan corporate headshot, editorial photograph: a white man in his mid-forties, a fractional chief marketing officer, wearing a slightly rumpled evergreen #0E2A23 knit polo, thousand-yard stare of a man who sold the top and feels nothing, warm bone paper #F6F3EC studio backdrop, soft even annual-report lighting, engraved old-money prospectus mood, one focal subject, head and shoulders centered. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

demo_screen_concept: An exchange trading terminal for receivables: a left-rail order book of open invoices quoted in cents on the dollar; a central candlestick chart of a selected client ticker (HNDRSN, BRNDCO, MPLWD) showing pay-probability price action with a stop-loss line; a right-rail positions panel of leveraged billable-hour positions with liquidation prices; a crawling top tape of client tickers; and a Panic Sell button that opens a confirmation modal whose risk disclosure makes things worse. Switching tickers redraws the SVG chart from mocked data; placing a limit order appends a row to the book.

design_direction.style: Bento-grid old-money exchange, bullion daylight mode. The page is staged like the prospectus site of a 140-year-old commodities exchange that has pivoted, without changing its tone of voice, to trading unpaid logo invoices. A warm bone-paper ground carries a strict bento lattice: flat tiles with 1px ink hairlines, 14px radii, and a single soft ambient shadow so shallow it reads as engraving rather than elevation. One deep banker's evergreen carries the wordmark, headlines, structural ink, and every institutional surface; one rationed bullion gold is reserved strictly for the single primary CTA, the live bid affordance, buy-side numbers, and the round 'est. 2026' seal, and appears almost nowhere else. Fraunces sets the wordmark and headlines in high-contrast old-style serif with the confidence of an engraved share certificate; Public Sans carries neutral, faintly governmental body copy. Every figure that pretends to be market data (bid/ask in cents on the dollar, pay-probability candles, leverage multiples, tier prices, spread percentages) is set in Spline Sans Mono with tabular figures inside tile chips so it reads like tape, not marketing. The hero's right tile is a live market snapshot: a client ticker, a sparkline, and a bid sitting at 61 cents. Motion is exchange-native and dry: the ticker tape scrolls at a fixed crawl, sparklines draw in once on scroll, numbers settle with a brief tick, tiles rise 4px on entry, the CTA presses down 1px, and everything is gated behind prefers-reduced-motion. No dark mode, no glass, no neon, no synthwave grids, no clay, no zine grain, no hard offset shadows. Reads like the kind of institution that would list your $850 invoice next to soybean futures and see no issue.

fonts.body: 'Public Sans', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif

design_direction.archetype: Institutional exchange prospectus: ticker-tape nav, split hero (serif claim + live market snapshot tile), bento feature lattice, fee-schedule pricing, market-commentary testimonials, closing CTA band styled as a settlement notice.
design_direction.motion_vocab: Fixed-crawl ticker tape, one-shot sparkline draw-ins, number tick-settles, 4px tile rise on scroll entry, 1px CTA press. All gated behind prefers-reduced-motion.
design_direction.hero_composition_notes: Two-column bento: left tile holds the Fraunces headline, sub, and the single gold CTA; right tile is a live market snapshot of ticker HNDRSN with a sparkline, bid/ask at 61/64 cents on the dollar, and a small 'list an invoice' secondary affordance. A slim tape of client tickers crawls above the fold.
